questions.scenario:
DevPolaris runs the production orders API behind an Application Load Balancer. Checkout traffic remains near 120 requests per minute, but customers report more failures after 09:40 UTC. The on-call engineer reads this target-group series:

```text
namespace: AWS/ApplicationELB
dimensions: LoadBalancer=app/orders-prod/123, TargetGroup=targetgroup/orders-api-prod/456
period: 1 minute
metric                         09:35  09:40  09:45
RequestCount Sum               118    121    126
HTTPCode_Target_5XX_Count Sum  0      8      39
TargetResponseTime p95         0.21s  0.84s  1.33s
```

Which conclusion is best supported before the engineer opens logs or traces?

options.text: The load balancer is generating the 5xx responses before selecting a target, so the engineer should inspect listener rules first.
Request growth overloaded the target group, so the engineer should add ECS tasks before checking utilization or dependencies.
Targets are returning more 5xx responses and responding more slowly under roughly stable traffic, but the metric does not yet identify the target-side cause.
One Availability Zone is failing, because the load-balancer and target-group dimensions identify the affected zone.

questions.explanation:
`HTTPCode_Target_5XX_Count` counts responses generated by targets, and the stable request count rules out a traffic surge as the immediate explanation. Rising target response time confirms degradation in the target path, but these dimensions do not identify an Availability Zone or distinguish application code from a dependency. `HTTPCode_ELB_5XX_Count` would describe load-balancer-generated errors.

Learn more in [Application Load Balancer metrics](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/application/load-balancer-cloudwatch-metrics.html).

questions.scenario: DevPolaris checkout errors rose immediately after a release. The service dashboard shows stable ALB request volume, rising target 5xx and p95 response time, three of three ECS tasks running with normal aggregate CPU and memory, and RDS connections plus write latency rising in the same window. Receipt Lambda errors remain at baseline. The on-call engineer needs the next evidence source that tests the strongest hypothesis without changing production. What should the engineer do?

options.text: Inspect ECS deployment events only, because a completed task count makes database evidence secondary.
Increase the RDS instance class, because aligned connection and latency movement is sufficient proof of insufficient capacity.
Open receipt Lambda logs, because checkout creates a receipt after an order is accepted.
Correlate affected orders API logs and traces with RDS query and connection evidence from the same time window.

questions.explanation:
Several independent signals point toward the database path, but correlation is not yet causation. Request-level logs or traces can connect the target 5xx to database operations, while RDS evidence can show pool pressure, slow queries, or saturation. Resizing before that check risks treating a query or connection-management problem as capacity, and the receipt Lambda stayed normal.

Learn more in [CloudWatch dashboards](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/monitoring/CloudWatch_Dashboards.html).

questions.scenario: The DevPolaris payments team wants an alarm specifically for failed `POST /v1/orders` requests. Native ALB target 5xx metrics are available only at load-balancer and target-group dimensions, while several routes share the orders target group. The application already emits structured route and outcome fields. The team needs route-level alerting without creating one CloudWatch time series per customer or request. Which design best meets the requirement?

options.text: Publish a custom checkout-failure metric with stable dimensions such as service and environment, then link the alarm to the dashboard and filtered application logs.
Add the route, customer ID, and request ID as dimensions to `HTTPCode_Target_5XX_Count` so the native ALB metric becomes request-specific.
Create one alarm per ECS task and infer the failed route from whichever task crosses the threshold.
Use only the native target-group 5xx alarm and label it `POST /v1/orders`, even though other routes share the target group.

questions.explanation:
A custom application metric can represent the business outcome that the native ALB dimensions cannot isolate. Stable dimensions such as service and environment keep cardinality bounded; customer and request IDs would create unbounded time series and cost. Per-task alarms do not express route outcome, and relabeling a target-group metric does not change its scope.

Learn more in [CloudWatch custom metrics](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/monitoring/publishingMetrics.html).

questions.scenario: DevPolaris is centralizing observability for production workloads in several AWS accounts. The operations team wants one monitoring account, fewer duplicate pages, and alarms that still express customer impact rather than isolated infrastructure movement. Which three design decisions support that operating model?

options.text: Link source accounts to the monitoring account so operators can view permitted metrics across accounts.
Copy every metric into a new custom namespace in the monitoring account so source-account identity is removed.
Use composite alarms to page only when lower-level conditions combine into the defined service-impact state.
Keep low-level alarms available for diagnosis while routing the customer-impact composite to the paging path.
Use account ID, request ID, and customer ID together as dimensions on every custom metric so central searches are exact.

questions.explanation:
Cross-account observability provides a central view without republishing every metric. Composite alarms can combine lower-level state into a page that matches service impact, while the component alarms remain useful during diagnosis. Replication adds unnecessary ownership and cost, and request or customer identifiers are high-cardinality metric dimensions.

Learn more in [CloudWatch cross-account observability](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/monitoring/CloudWatch-Unified-Cross-Account.html) and [composite alarms](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/monitoring/Create_Composite_Alarm.html).

questions.scenario: DevPolaris has an SQS worker that normally receives messages only during business hours. The team proposes an alarm on `ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage`, but the metric has no datapoint overnight when the queue is empty. Paging on missing data would create false incidents, while ignoring a real backlog during business hours would violate the processing objective. How should the alarm be configured?

options.text: Treat missing data as breaching so the absence of overnight queue metrics pages the on-call engineer.
Choose missing-data behavior that represents an empty queue as healthy, and evaluate age over a sustained window when datapoints exist.
Replace queue age with ECS CPU because CPU emits continuously and therefore cannot have missing-data ambiguity.
Use one single-period threshold and suppress notifications on a fixed overnight schedule.

questions.explanation:
Missing data must be interpreted according to what absence means for that metric. For an inactive empty queue, absence should not become a failure, while a sustained age threshold during actual work still detects backlog. ECS CPU does not measure the queue objective, and a one-period alarm plus schedule suppression can hide delayed work outside expected hours.

Learn more in [Configuring how CloudWatch alarms treat missing data](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/monitoring/AlarmThatSendsEmail.html#ConfiguringHowCloudWatchAlarmsTreatMissingData).

questions.scenario: During an orders-service incident, the DevPolaris dashboard opens with S3 bucket size, internal queue shard identifiers, and staging Lambda duration. Checkout request rate, error rate, and latency appear below the fold, followed by ECS task health and RDS pressure. Responders lose several minutes deciding whether customers are affected. Which dashboard change best supports triage?

options.text: Place ECS and RDS utilization first because infrastructure capacity should be understood before customer symptoms.
Order rows alphabetically by AWS service so responders follow a consistent console navigation sequence.
Keep the current order and add a red annotation whenever the checkout alarm fires.
Lead with checkout traffic, errors, and latency, then show ingress, runtime, dependencies, async work, and runbook context.

questions.explanation:
A triage dashboard should answer whether users are affected before it asks which component is responsible. Layering ingress, runtime, dependencies, and async work below the service view lets responders move from impact to cause. Alphabetical service ordering and annotations do not fix the information hierarchy.

Learn more in [CloudWatch dashboards](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/monitoring/CloudWatch_Dashboards.html).

questions.scenario: DevPolaris ECS CPU follows a predictable weekday pattern: it reaches 75 percent for one minute at 09:00, returns to 45 percent, and has not correlated with latency, errors, or task loss. A static 70 percent alarm paged 46 times in 30 days and responders now wait for it to clear. The team still needs notification when CPU behavior is unusual and service health degrades. Which redesign is best?

options.text: Use anomaly detection or a sustained baseline-aware CPU condition, combine it with service-health evidence where appropriate, and document the owner and first response.
Raise the static threshold to 90 percent and keep the one-minute evaluation because fewer pages will restore trust.
Remove CPU from all dashboards and page only on ALB 5xx, because capacity signals cannot contribute to diagnosis.
Keep the 70 percent alarm and convert each page into an unowned daily ticket so on-call interruption stops.

questions.explanation:
The current threshold mistakes a predictable pattern for an incident. Baseline-aware or sustained evaluation can detect unusual behavior, and combining it with service-health evidence makes paging more meaningful. Raising one static threshold does not address the pattern, deleting capacity evidence harms diagnosis, and changing delivery without ownership does not make the signal actionable.

Learn more in [CloudWatch anomaly detection](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/monitoring/CloudWatch_Anomaly_Detection.html).
